coredata.dc:title: Magnetic fluorescent probe of hydroxylated-halloysite and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in molecularly imprinted polymer to enrich and determine marbofloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Pongprom, Angkana
Chansud, Nutnicha
Sa-nguanprang, Surisa
Jullakan, Sirintorn
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent probe was fabricated and applied to determine marbofloxacin. The fluorescent probe integrated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, hydroxylated-halloysite, and magnetite nanoparticles into molecularly imprinted polymer (OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP). The determination of marbofloxacin was performed by measuring the reduction in the fluorescence emission of the probe when marbofloxacin bound to the recognition sites of the MIP. The intensity of the nanoprobe was quenched linearly from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1 and the limit of detection was 0.03 μg L-1. The fluorescent nanoprobe was selective for marbofloxacin with did not quenched fluorescence emission by other similar structure compounds. The fabricated magnetic probe was applied to determine marbofloxacin in milk. Good recoveries from 94.2 to 99.6 % were achieved and RSDs were<5 %. The accuracy of the proposed probe was good, and results were in good agreement with results from chromatographic analysis. The developed method exhibited high sensitivity and a fast response. The analysis is simple and cheap to perform, environmentally friendly and can be applied to determine trace contaminants in various food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Marbofloxacin
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Hydroxylated-halloysite
Selective polymer
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of silica modified magnetite nanoparticles
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Synthesis of the nanocomposite fluorescent probe
Effect of template (TM) to monomer (MN) to cross-linker (CL) ratio
Effect of amount of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles
Comparison of different nanoprobes for the determination of marbofloxacin
Comparison of analytical methods of marbofloxacin determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No SCI6601316S), and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC) of the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for proofreading the text.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedure of the magnetic nanocomposite OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe for the determination of marbofloxacin.

Emission spectra are of the proposed OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP fluorescent probe before (a), and after (b) template removal and of an OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP fluorescent probe (c) (A). TEM images are of N-GQDs, the inset is particle size distribution (B) and OH-HNTs (C). SEM images are of OH-HNTs (D) and the proposed OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP nanoprobe (E).

Incubation time increased the fluorescence quenching by marbofloxacin of the proposed OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe (A). F and F0 are the emission intensity of the magnetic nanocomposite probe with and without marbofloxacin, respectively. The charts show the effect on sensitivity of the TM: MN: CL ratio (B), dispersion solution pH (C) and loading of OH-HNTs in the probe (D). Sensitivity was calculated from the slope of calibration curves plotted between emission intensity and marbofloxacin concentration from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1.

Fluorescence spectra are of the proposed OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe (A) and the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP probe (B) in the presence of various concentrations of marbofloxacin. The calibration curves (C) show the linearity of response of the proposed probe, and the photograph shows the proposed magnetic composite probe in the absence (left) and presence of marbofloxacin (D).

Selectivity of the proposed OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP fluorescent probe (A) and the chemical structures of marbofloxacin and other structural analogues used in the selectivity study (B). Competitive study of the determination of marbofloxacin by the proposed OH-HNTs@ N-GQDs@ Fe3O4@MIP fluorescent probe in the presence of levofloxacin (n = 3) (C). The chart shows the sensitivity of different magnetic nanocomposite probes for the determination of marbofloxacin. The sensitivity was calculated from the slope of a calibration curve plotted between emission intensity and marbofloxacin concentrations from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1 (D). Stability (E) and reproducibility (F) of the proposed OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@ Fe3O4@MIP probe (n = 3).
The determination of marbofloxacin in milk using the proposed magnetic nanocomposite OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe (n = 3).
The analytical methods for the determination of marbofloxacin in various samples.

head.ce:title: Magnetic fluorescent probe of hydroxylated-halloysite and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in molecularly imprinted polymer to enrich and determine marbofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent probe was fabricated and applied to determine marbofloxacin. The fluorescent probe integrated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, hydroxylated-halloysite, and magnetite nanoparticles into molecularly imprinted polymer (OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP). The determination of marbofloxacin was performed by measuring the reduction in the fluorescence emission of the probe when marbofloxacin bound to the recognition sites of the MIP. The intensity of the nanoprobe was quenched linearly from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1 and the limit of detection was 0.03 μg L-1. The fluorescent nanoprobe was selective for marbofloxacin with did not quenched fluorescence emission by other similar structure compounds. The fabricated magnetic probe was applied to determine marbofloxacin in milk. Good recoveries from 94.2 to 99.6 % were achieved and RSDs were<5 %. The accuracy of the proposed probe was good, and results were in good agreement with results from chromatographic analysis. The developed method exhibited high sensitivity and a fast response. The analysis is simple and cheap to perform, environmentally friendly and can be applied to determine trace contaminants in various food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Marbofloxacin is a quinolone antibiotic that is generally used in veterinary medicine to prevent intramammary infections in dairy cattle. However, the excessive use of marbofloxacin can leave residues in milk and dairy products [1]. Residual antibiotics in foods can cause allergic reactions and promote antibiotic resistance in foodborne pathogens [2]. The European Union has set a maximum residue limit (MRL) for marbofloxacin in raw milk of 30 μg kg−1. Therefore, the monitoring of marbofloxacin levels in the relevant food is a necessity.

For trace residue determination of marbofloxacin in complex samples such as milk and dairy products, a highly sensitive and selective analytical method is generally preferred. Since the determination of marbofloxacin has been based on separation and detection by chromatographic methods [3–5], expensive instruments, personal expertise and large volumes of organic solvent are required. Fluorescence spectroscopy is a technique that could improve the sensitivity and selectivity of marbofloxacin determination if used with a nanocomposite fluorescent probe. Fluorescent quantum dots have been developed as probes to determine various analytes such as salicylic acid [6], copper ions [7] fluoride ions [8] and tetracycline [9]. Inorganic quantum dot and carbon-based quantum dots have both been successfully applied in the fabrication of sensing probes, but the non-toxicity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) makes them a better candidate material [9]. A highly selective nanocomposite fluorescent probe could be fabricated by combining nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with a highly selective material like molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). MIP is tailor-made receptor and can be easily produced using the analyte as a template in the presence of a functional monomer and a cross-linker. Once the polymerization process is completed and the template removed, highly specific cavities are produced which can rebind to the analyte in a sample solution, while other compounds in the sample cannot [10]. Created three dimensional imprinted cavities are complementary to the shape, size and functionality of the target analyte [11]. The advantages of MIP including ease of preparation, low cost, high chemical and physical stability, considerable reusability and high selectivity [12,13] MIP has been used as a selective material in solid phase extraction [14,15], solid phase microextraction [16], electrode modification [17], optical sensors [18] and in paper-based microfluidics [13]. Several synthetic strategies have been designed and developed to produce MIP [19]. Among various strategies, sol–gel polymerization is an interesting method due to the reaction can occur in eco-friendly and at mild temperatures [19,20]. In addition, high affinity materials can be integrated into the fluorescent probe to improve binding with the target analyte. To determine marbofloxacin in complex samples, hydroxylated-halloysite nanotubes (OH-HNTs) could be incorporated. HNTs have previously been used as an affinity material to aid adsorption [21,22]. For the determination of marbofloxacin at trace levels in complex samples, a preconcentration procedure can enhance the enrichment factor of the system. Incorporating magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4) is a simple modification that enables the rapid isolation of the probe from the sample solution with a magnet after binding to the target is complete [23].

In this work, a composite probe of hydroxylated-halloysite nanotubes, magnetite nanoparticles and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots incorporated in a molecularly imprinted polymer (OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP) was synthesized and used for the enhancement and determination of marbofloxacin. The nanoprobe was characterized, and the determination condition and synthesis procedure were optimized. The analytical performances of the developed sensor were also investigated and the analysis of real samples was compared with the conventional HPLC method.

Marbofloxacin, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 96.0 %), citric acid (98.0 %) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 98.0 %) were from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., ltd., Japan. FeCl2·4H2O and FeCl3·6H2O were from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Halloysite nanoclay was from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) was from Ajax Finechem, Australia. Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (THAM), Ammonium solution (NH4OH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets and acetonitrile were from RCI Labscan, Thailand.

Fluorescence response was measured using a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). UV–vis spectra were obtained with a UV-2600i UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Surface area analysis followed the Branauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (ASAP 2460 Micromeristic, USA). The morphology of nanocomposite probes and related materials was investigated by JEM-2010 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, Japan) and JEM-5200 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEOL, Japan). Functional groups of fabricated nanocomposite fluorescent probes and related materials were investigated by GX Fourier transform infrared (GX FTIR) spectroscope (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silica modified magnetite nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Silica modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized following a reported method [24] and the detail is provided in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were prepared according to a previous report [25]. First, in a 50 mL round bottom flask, 2.0 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (THAM) were heated to 225 οC, using a heating mantle, at which point the mixture became a pale-yellow color. After leaving the mixture to cool to room temperature (27 ± 1 °C), the obtained product was a brown solid. The synthesized N-GQDs was dispersed in 5.0 mL of DI water and the solution was adjusted to pH neutral with 0.025 M NaOH. The synthesized N-GQDs were kept at 4 οC until used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the nanocomposite fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The magnetic OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was synthesized in a sol–gel polymerization. In a brown bottle, 9.0 mg of marbofloxacin, 5.0 mL of ethanol and 47 μL of APTES were mixed and stirred for 30 min. Then, 0.70 mg of OH-HNTs, 5.0 mL of N-GQDs, 344 μL of TEOS, 150 μL of NH4OH and 10 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were added, and stirring continued for 4 h. The synthesized magnetic nanocomposite probe was then washed with 10.0 mL of ethanol to remove template and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. To completely remove all template from the probe, the washing and spinning was repeated three times. The synthesized OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was then dried at 60 οC and kept in a desiccator. A nanocomposite non-imprinted polymer (OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP) was also synthesized by the same method but without the addition of marbofloxacin. The fabrication strategy of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP fluorescent probe for marbofloxacin detection is showed in Fig. 1 .

The OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was dispersed in 5.0 mL of sample solution under stirring at 500 rpm for 20 min to completely adsorb the target molecules. The nanoprobe was separated from the sample with a magnet. To determine marbofloxacin, the separated probe was dispersed in 100 μL of 10 mM citrate–phosphate buffer (pH 6), and fluorescence was measured using an excitation wavelength of 335 nm and emission was measured from 350 to 600 nm, the slit width was 10 nm. The analysis was performed at 25 οC.

All milk samples were obtained from the local market in Hat Yai, Songkhla, Thailand. Milk samples were pretreated as follows: 10.0 mL of milk sample was placed in a 50 mL polypropylene tube and centrifuged for 10 min at 5500 rpm. The supernatant was isolated, added to 10.0 mL of acetonitrile and vigorously shaken for 5 min. The mixture solution was centrifuged at 5500 rpm for 20 min at room temperature. The supernatant was separated and evaporated at 60 °C. The evaporated sample was dissolved in 5.0 mL of ultrapure water and mixed with the magnetic nanocomposite probes for the determination of marbofloxacin. HPLC condition for the determination of marbofloxacin is given in Supplementary Material.

Before using the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe to determine marbofloxacin in real samples, the complete removal of template molecules was evaluated by measuring the intensity of the fluorescence response of the probe. Before removal of the template, fluorescence emission was low but after removal, fluorescence emission recovered to the same level exhibited by the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP probe (Fig. 2
A). These results demonstrated that the marbofloxacin templates were completely removed from the probe, which could then be used to determine marbofloxacin in real samples without memory effect.

Functional groups of N-GQDs, OH-HNTs, Fe3O4@SiO2, and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. In the spectrum of N-GQDs ( Fig. S1 curve a), the peaks at 3422 and 2913 cm−1 were due to the –OH/–NH and CH stretching, respectively. The stretching vibration of CC from the aromatic ring was detected at 1578 cm− 1, and the typical signals at 1405 and 1272 cm− 1 were attributed to C-NH-C and CO stretching vibrations. The spectrum of OH-HNTs (curve b) presented absorption peaks at 3692 and 3621 cm− 1 attributed to inner –OH groups. The peak at 911 cm−1 was caused by the inner surface vibration of –OH groups, whereas the peak at 1032 cm−1 was due to Si-O-Si stretching vibration. The spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2 (curve c) showed broad bands of OH stretching and Si-O-Si symmetric vibration at 3397 and 1085 cm− 1, respectively. Absorption peaks of Fe-O-Fe from Fe3O4 were present at around 588 and 469 cm−1. The spectrum of OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP (curve d) demonstrated that N-GQDs, OH-HNTs, and Fe3O4@SiO2 were successfully incorporated into the MIP.

TEM images of N-GQDs and OH-HNTs are showed Fig. 2 B and Fig. 2 C, respectively. The result revealed that the N-GQDs were uniformly spherical with the sizes between 2 and 6 nm, with an average size of 3.7 nm. Fig. 2 D and Fig. 2 E illustrated the SEM images of OH-HNTs and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe, respectively. The synthesized OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP demonstrated rough surface of recognition cavities which can be rebind with marbofloxacin.

The surface areas and pore structure of OH-HNTs and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP were measured via the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms. The isotherms of OH-HNTs (Fig. S2 A) and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP (Fig. S2 B) were type-IV isotherms, indicating the existence of mesopores and macropores. The BET surface areas of OH-HNTs and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP were 47.93 and 59.94 m2/g, respectively. The magnetic nanocomposite MIP probe therefore had a large surface area for the specific adsorption of marbofloxacin.

The quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs defines their emission efficiency. The comparative method was applied to determine QY of the N-GQDs and the nanocomposite probes by comparison with quinine sulfate. In this work, the QY is calculated using the slope of the line generated by plotting the integrated fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 335 nm against the absorption at 335 nm for different concentrations of the N-GQDs, nanocomposite probes and quinine sulfate solutions. The QYs of N-GQDs, N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP were calculated according to the following equation [26];
ϕ x
=
ϕ
ST
×
Gra
d x
/ G r a
d
ST
×
η x
/
η
ST
2
where ϕ x and ϕ ST are the QY of the target compound and quinine sulfate, respectively. The QY of quinine sulfate is 54 %. Gradx and GradST are the gradient from the linear regression analysis of the target compound and quinine sulfate, respectively. η x and η ST are the solvent refractive indices of the target compound and quinine sulfate, and the refractive index of water is 1.33. The calculated QYs of N-GQDs, N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP were 59.77, 49.26, and 45.06 %, respectively. These results demonstrated the strong fluorescence emission efficiency of the N-GQDs and the nanocomposite probe, which should, therefore enable the fabrication of a highly sensitive optosensor.

In order to maximize the analytical performances of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe, the parameters optimized included the incubation time between the probe and marbofloxacin, the ratio of components used in the synthesis of the probe, the dispersion solution pH, and the amount of OH-HNTs used in the synthesis of the probe.

The binding time between marbofloxacin and the probe affects the determination of marbofloxacin. The effect of binding time was evaluated from 2 to 30 min. The results demonstrated that the interaction of marbofloxacin and the recognition cavities was impacted by time (Fig. 3
A). As the binding time increased, quenching efficiency (F0/F) increased and reached an almost constant state after 16 min. Thus, the optimal binding time between marbofloxacin and the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP fluorescent probe was 16 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of template (TM) to monomer (MN) to cross-linker (CL) ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The mole ratio of TM to MN to CL is an important criterion for the determination of an analyte since it can affect the number of recognition sites and physical properties of the probe. The optimal mole ratio of TM: MN: CL was assessed from the sensitivity of the response of the probe toward marbofloxacin. The best sensitivity was obtained using a probe synthesized at a TM: MN: CL mole ratio of 1: 8: 60 (Fig. 3 B). Increasing the ratio of MN above 8 reduced sensitivity because excess MN hindered the formation of recognition sites. However, at a mole ratio of MN lower than 8, sensitivity decreased due to the reduced number of recognition sites formed. In addition, when the ratio of CL was more than 60, the sensitivity of the probe decreased because the excess CL obscured recognition sites. At a CL ratio lower than 60, sensitivity decreased because the polymer was unstable, which produced defective binding sites. Therefore, the mole ratio of TM: MN: CL of 1: 8: 60 was selected for the fabrication of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe to determine marbofloxacin.

The effect of dispersion solution pH on the sensitivity of the probe toward marbofloxacin was investigated using citrate–phosphate buffer solution pH-adjusted from 5.0 to 8.0. The sensitivity of the nanocomposite OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was highest at pH 6.0 (Fig. 3 C). Protonation or deprotonation of marbofloxacin molecules disrupts hydrogen bonding, reducing the interaction between the probe and the analyte. At pH above 6.0, sensitivity decreased as the analyte deprotonated, reducing hydrogen bonding and interactions between marbofloxacin and recognition sites. At pH lower than 6.0, sensitivity decreased because electrostatic repulsion prevented hydrogen bonding. Thus, the magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe was dispersed in a buffer at pH 6.

The optimal amount of OH-HNTs incorporated in the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was investigated from 1.0 to 9.0 mg. The probe fabricated using 5.0 mg of OH-HNTs exhibited the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3 D). Higher amounts of OH-HNTs blocked imprinted sites, resulting in decreased sensitivity. Smaller amounts of OH-HNTs produced too few adsorption sites, resulting in lower sensitivity. Therefore, the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was fabricated using 5.0 mg of OH-HNTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles

ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performances of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe for the determination of marbofloxacin were compared with the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP probe. The emission intensity of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe clearly decreased with increments of marbofloxacin concentration from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1, whereas the response of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP probe slightly decreased in the same concentration range (Fig. 4 A and B). Marbofloxacin could bind with recognition sites of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe but there were no recognition sites on the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP probe. Therefore, only non-specific adsorption could occur, resulting in only a small quenching of fluorescence intensity.

The OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe produced good linearity of response from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1 (Fig. 4 C). The linear regression equation was y (F0/F) = (0.243 ± 0.003)Xmarbofloxacin + (1.15 ± 0.04) (R2 = 0.9944). The limit of detection (LOD) (based on 3SD/Slope) and limit of quantification (LOQ) (based on 10SD/Slope) were 0.03 μg L-1 and 0.10 μg L-1, respectively, the detail of calculation is provided in supporting material. The LOD value is much lower than the MRL due to the high sensitivity of the proposed sensor. A photograph of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe in the presence and absence of marbofloxacin under UV light is shown in Fig. 4 D.

The selectivity of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was evaluated by comparing the sensitivity of response toward marbofloxacin and the structurally analogous compounds lomefloxacin, ciprofloxacin, ofloxacin, and levofloxacin. The developed nanocomposite probe was dispersed in 5.0 mL of spiked water solution in the presence of individual marbofloxacin and the selected structurally analogous compounds, the determination procedure as described in section 2.6.The highest sensitivity was obtained for the determination of marbofloxacin (Fig. 5
A), indicating that the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was highly specific toward marbofloxacin (Fig. 5 B). To confirm the selectivity of the developed probe, a competitive study was conducted. The OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was used to determine marbofloxacin in the presence of various concentrations of levofloxacin (Fig. 5 C). The sensitivity of the response of the probe to marbofloxacin was unchanged when the concentration of levofloxacin was increased. The results showed that the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was highly specific and selective for marbofloxacin. Other compounds that may be contained in real samples did not interfere with the determination of marbofloxacin. The results of these studies indicated that the developed magnetic nanocomposite probe could determine marbofloxacin in complex samples without a complicated sample pre-treatment procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different nanoprobes for the determination of marbofloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentrations of marbofloxacin were determined using various formulations of the magnetic composite probe containing the synthesized N-GQDs. The sensitivity of the different probes toward marbofloxacin was studied. The probe formulations were N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP, OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP, N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP, and OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP (Fig. 5D). The N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was less sensitive than the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe because there were fewer adsorption sites for marbofloxacin. The OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP and N-GQDs@Fe3O4@NIP also exhibited poor sensitivity due to the lack of recognition sites for marbofloxacin. Therefore, the magnetic nanocomposite OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was used to determine marbofloxacin in real samples.

The stability of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was investigated by monitoring the fluorescence emission intensity over time. The probe was dispersed in citrate–phosphate buffer solution at pH 6.0. The emission intensity of the probe remained unchanged for 300 min (Fig. 5 E), which is much enough time to determine marbofloxacin by spectrofluorimetry. This result indicated the good stability of the nanoprobe. The reproducibility of the OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe for the determination of marbofloxacin was also evaluated. Six different fabrication lots were prepared and used to determine marbofloxacin and the RSD of average sensitivity was 1.5 %, indicating the good reproducibility ( Fig. 5 F).

The nanocomposite OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP probe was then used to determine marbofloxacin in milk. In some samples, concentrations of marbofloxacin were found that were below the MRL, the quantitative analysis was performed using external calibration. The accuracy was then evaluated with a spike and recovery test. Recoveries of marbofloxacin ranged from 94.2 to 99.6 %, RSDs < 5 % (Table 1 ). Amount of marbofloxacin in spiked samples were also determined by HPLC. A chromatogram of a milk and spiked milk samples are showed in Fig. S5 A. The results achieved from the developed method and HPLC were in good agreement (Fig. S5 B). This evaluation confirmed that the fabricated optosensor using the nanocomposite fluorescent probe can be applied to determine marbofloxacin in milk. The probe also has the potential to determine other analytes due to its tunable selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of analytical methods of marbofloxacin determination
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed optosensor exhibited better sensitivity than methods reported in previous works. Recoveries and precision were comparable or better (Table 2 ). Compared to a reported chromatographic method, the fabricated optosensor showed better sensitivity and selectivity. Also, the analysis cost of the developed optosensor is much lower and the pre-analytical sample preparation is quick and simple. Moreover, liquid chromatography consumes large volumes of organic solvent for the mobile phase. The proposed optosensor has good selectivity for marbofloxacin without requirement of an analytical column. One limitation of the developed optosensor is that it can only determine a single target analyte whereas chromatographic techniques can simultaneously determine several target compounds.

A magnetic nanocomposite fluorescent probe was fabricated for the determination of marbofloxacin. The nanocomposite probe was produced by embedding hydroxylated-halloysite, graphene quantum dots and magnetite nanoparticles in molecularly imprinted polymer (OH-HNTs@N-GQDs@Fe3O4@MIP). The fabricated nanoprobe was applied to quantitatively determine marbofloxacin in milk and good accuracy was achieved. The results of analysis with the developed optosensor agreed well with HPLC, but the fabricated optosensor had higher sensitivity, is simpler to perform and requires lower cost instrument. The developed optosensor could be used for the sensitive determination of other target analytes with minor modification of the probe.

Angkana Pongprom: Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Nutnicha Chansud: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Surisa Sa-nguanprang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Sirintorn Jullakan: Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Opas Bunkoed: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No SCI6601316S), and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC) of the Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for proofreading the text.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108389.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
